title: Mixed Question on AP and GP

concepts.title: Some Solved Problem on Arithmetic and Geometric Mean
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved examples 

Q) Consider the following function : $f\left( x,y,z \right)=\frac{x}{y}+\sqrt{\frac{y}{z}}+\sqrt[3]{\frac{z}{x}}$  for $x,y$ and $z$ all positive real numbers. Suppose we wish to find the minimum value of this function. Rewriting a bit, and applying the AM-GM inequality, we have : 

$\begin{array}{l} f\left( x,y,z \right) & ~=6\cdot \frac{\frac{x}{y}+\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{y}{z}}+\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{y}{z}}+\frac{1}{3}\sqrt[3]{\frac{z}{x}}+\frac{1}{3}\sqrt[3]{\frac{z}{x}}+\frac{1}{3}\sqrt[3]{\frac{z}{x}}}{6} \\ {} & ~\ge 6\sqrt[6]{\frac{x}{y}\cdot \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{y}{z}}\cdot +\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{y}{z}}\cdot \frac{1}{3}\sqrt[3]{\frac{z}{x}}\cdot \frac{1}{3}\sqrt[3]{\frac{z}{x}}\cdot \frac{1}{3}\sqrt[3]{\frac{z}{x}}} \\ {} & ~=6\sqrt[6]{\frac{1}{2.2.3.3.3}\frac{\text{xyz}}{\text{yz}}} \\ {} & ~={{2}^{2/3}}\cdot {{3}^{1/2}} \\ \end{array}$

Further, we know that the two sides are equal exactly when all the terms of the mean are equal :

$\text{f}\left( \text{x},\text{y},\text{z} \right)={{2}^{2/3}}\cdot {{3}^{1/2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ when }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{x}}{\text{y}}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\text{y}}{\text{z}}}=\frac{1}{3}\sqrt[3]{\frac{\text{z}}{\text{x}}}$

Q) If $A$ and $G$ are arithmetic mean (AM) and geometric mean (GM) between two numbers a and $b$ find the roots of the equation : ${{x}^{2}}-2Ax+{{G}^{2}}$ 

Solution : 

Let $\alpha $ and $\beta $ be the roots of the equation, then

$\alpha +\beta =2\text{ A}~\And~ \alpha \beta ={{\text{G}}^{2}}$

Also, $\text{A}$ is the AM between a & b and G is GM between a & b

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow & \text{A}=\frac{\text{a}+\text{b}}{2}\And \text{G}=\sqrt{\text{a}b} \\ \Rightarrow & \alpha +\beta =\text{a}+\text{b }\text{ and }\alpha \beta =\text{ab} \\ \end{array}$

$\Rightarrow$ The roots are a $\And$ b.

Q) If $a,b,c$ are in A.P., $x$ is the GM of $a,b$ and $y$ is GM of $b,c$, show that ${{b}^{2}}$ is the AM of ${{x}^{2}}$ and ${{\text{y}}^{2}}$. 

Solution : 

a, b, c are in AP $\Rightarrow 2b=a+c$ (i)

$\text{x}$ is G.M. of $\text{a},\text{b} \Rightarrow \text{ x}=\sqrt{\text{ab}}$ (ii)

$\text{y}$ is G.M. of b, c $\Rightarrow \text{ y}=\sqrt{\text{bc}}$ (iii)

Squaring (ii) & (iii) and adding, we get

$\Rightarrow {{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}=\text{ab}+\text{bc}=\text{b}\left( \text{a}+\text{c} \right)$

From (i) $a+c=2b$

$\Rightarrow {{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}=2{{\text{b}}^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow {{\text{b}}^{2}}=\frac{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}}{2}$

Hence ${{b}^{2}}$ is arithmetic mean (AM) of ${{x}^{2}}$ and ${{y}^{2}}$.

Q) If $0<r<1$ and $m$ is a positive integer, show that $\left( 2m+1 \right){{r}^{2m}}\left( 1-r \right)<1-{{r}^{2m+1}}$ 

Solution : 

Using AM > GM, we have

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} & \frac{1+\text{r}+{{\text{r}}^{2}}+\ldots .+{{\text{r}}^{2\text{ m}}}}{2\text{ m}+1}>{{\left( 1.r.{{r}^{2}}\ldots ..{{\text{r}}^{2\text{ m}}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{2\text{ m}-1}}} \\ {} & \text{ or }\frac{1-\text{r}{{2}^{\text{m}+1}}}{\left( 1-\text{r} \right)\left( 2\text{ m}+1 \right)}>{{\left( 1.r.{{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{2\text{ m}+1}}} \\ {} & \text{ or }\frac{1-{{\text{r}}^{2\text{ m}+1}}}{\left( 1-\text{r} \right)\left( 2\text{ m}+1 \right)}>{{\text{r}}^{\frac{2\text{ m}\left( 2\text{ m}+1 \right)}{2\text{ m}+1}}}\text{ or }1-{{\text{r}}^{2\text{ m}+1}}>\left( 1-\text{r} \right)\left( 2\text{ m}+1 \right){{\text{r}}^{2\text{ m}}} \\ \end{array}$
